thread.title: Exhibit honors retired Mississippi State Representative
thread.title_full: Exhibit honors retired Mississippi State Representative

author: Christopher Fields
title: Exhibit honors retired Mississippi State Representative
text: The trailblazer received another significant honor Friday. JACKSON, Miss. (WLBT) - A new exhibit was unveiled for Alyce G. Clarke at the Smith Roberson Museum, highlighting her phenomenal work and contributions to the state and the country. The audience was wowed after the Connecting the Dots Foundation pulled back the sheets of the stunning bust in Alyce G. Clarke’s honor. It was created by award-winning professional sculptor Harold Millier. “I hope it’s something the city will enjoy and appreciate. The fact that some people will work and just know your work will pay off, and to me, this is my work really paying off,” said retired Mississippi House of Representative Alyce G. Clarke. Memorable photos were also part of the exhibit. It also includes the dress the icon wore when sworn into office in 1985. Clarke was joined by state, county, and city officials who witnessed the big moment. “I’m most grateful that we have the pleasure to have this exhibit while we still have you. Your city still loves you,” said Mayor Chokwe Antar Lumumba of Jackson. “When she won the election, it was my job to work with her and support her for the betterment of our district, our city, and this state,” said Sen. Sollie D. Norwood of District 28. Clarke is the first African American woman elected to the Mississippi House of Representatives. She’s the longest-working female who served in that role. Throughout the span of her career, the icon authored more than fifteen hundred bills. “I’m so overwhelmed. I’m just so honored. I had no idea that anything like this could happen. What’s so good to me is that I’m still alive to see it. These are the kinds of things that people don’t see happen. This is a blessing. I’m just hoping that other young people will see what they can do if you just try. I’m just so grateful,” said Clarke. After serving for 38 years, the trailblazer for change retired back in January. A portrait of Clarke is permanently displayed at the State Capitol building in her honor. Want more WLBT news in your inbox? Click here to subscribe to our newsletter.
